tldr: Stark catches accessibility issues at design time inside Figma; Assistiv Labs lets QA test built UIs with real screen readers in the cloud. The two cover different stages of the lifecycle, so a switch usually means shifting investment from design-phase checks to runtime AT QA.
why_switch: Test on real NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver remotely
No need to ship AT licenses to distributed QA
Catch runtime ARIA bugs missed by design tools
Cross-platform AT access from one browser
Better fit for engineering-led a11y QA
what_to_export: Vision simulation screenshots used in reviews
Design-system a11y guidelines built on Stark
export_method: Stark stores annotations and reports inside Figma files. Open each design file and export Stark-generated reports or screenshots; many findings are already captured as Figma comments or layers, so simply preserving the Figma file history is enough. Pull any standalone reports out of the Stark plugin via its export options. Re-document conventions (severity, WCAG mapping) internally since these belong to your team. There is no large database to migrate. Keep Stark installed during the transition so designers can still run quick checks while the runtime QA flow is set up.
import_method: Sign up for Assistiv Labs and provision seats for the QA engineers who will run AT testing. Train them on launching screen reader sessions (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver) against staging or preview URLs. Add an AT QA pass to your release checklist, focused on flows where Stark's design-time checks cannot catch ARIA, focus, or live-region bugs. Tie findings into the same tracker your team already uses. Decide whether to retain Stark for designers (recommended) and use Assistiv Labs purely for engineering-side QA, or reduce Stark to a free tier if budget pressure was the motivation.

concept_mapping.target: No like-for-like; keep design-time tooling separately
Verified live with screen reader on Assistiv Labs

effort: Days to set up seats and process; weeks to build durable AT QA habits.
gotchas: Assistiv Labs is runtime-only; you lose design-phase checks if you drop Stark fully
Per-seat pricing scales with QA team size
Streaming latency can affect screen reader timing perception
Real disabled-user testing still recommended, this tool is for QA simulation
Training time required to use screen readers correctly
Some AT versions/locales may not be available remotely

email.subject: Shifting a11y QA spend from Stark to Assistiv Labs
email.body:
Hi Stark team,

The accessibility checks that bite us most are runtime ARIA, focus, and screen-reader bugs that surface after a build is shipped, so we are shifting investment toward Assistiv Labs for QA-side AT testing. We plan to wind down our Stark plan over the next 14 days.

We still see value in design-time checks, so depending on how the transition goes we may stay on a free tier rather than fully cancelling. We will confirm by the end of the 14-day window.

Could you confirm:
- Cancellation steps and final billing date if we move off the paid plan
- Whether plugin-generated reports remain accessible afterward
- Any free tier limits we should know about for designers who stay

Thanks for the help getting our designers on board with a11y.

Your customer
